The Seventh Passage. Who May Be Called Immaculate. How It is that in God’s Sight No Man is Justified.
title: The Seventh Passage. Who May Be Called Immaculate. How It is that in God’s Sight No Man is Justified.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: How It is that in God’s Sight No Man is Justified.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: “They also, says he, “quote the text: ‘For in thy sight shall no man living be justified.’”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: And his affected answer to this passage amounts to nothing else than the showing how texts of Holy Scripture seem to clash with one another, whereas it is our duty rather to demonstrate their agreement.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: These are his words: “We must confront them with this answer, from the testimony of the evangelist concerning holy Zacharias and Elisabeth, when he says, ‘And they were both righteous before God, walking in all the commandments and ordinances of the Lord blameless.’”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Now both these righteous persons had, of course, read amongst these very commandments the method of cleansing their own sins.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: For, according to what is said in the Epistle to the Hebrews of “every high priest taken from among men,” Zacharias used no doubt to offer sacrifices even for his own sins.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The meaning, however, of the phrase “blameless,” which is applied to him, we have already, as I suppose, sufficiently explained.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: “And,” he adds, “the blessed apostle says, ‘That we should be holy, and without blame before Him.’”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: This, according to him, is said that we should be so, if those persons are to be understood by “blameless” who are altogether without sin.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: If, however, they are “blameless” who are without blame or censure, then it is impossible for us to deny that there have been, and still are, such persons even in this present life; for it does not follow that a man is without sin because he has not a blot of accusation.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: Accordingly the apostle, when selecting ministers for ordination, does not say, “If any be sinless,” for he would be unable to find any such; but he says, “If any be without accusation,” for such, of course, he would be able to find.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: But our opponent does not tell us how, in accordance with his views, we ought to understand the scripture, “For in Thy sight shall no man living be justified.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: The meaning of these words is plain enough, receiving as it does additional light from the preceding clause: “Enter not,” says the Psalmist, “into judgment with Thy servant, for in Thy sight shall no man living be justified.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: It is judgment which he fears, therefore he desires that mercy which triumphs over judgment.

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: For the meaning of the prayer, “Enter not into judgment with Thy servant,” is this: “Judge me not according to Thyself,” who art without sin; “for in Thy sight shall no man living be justified.”

verses.book: A Treatise Concerning Man’s Perfection in Righteousness.
verses.text: This without doubt is understood as spoken of the present life, whilst the predicate “shall not be justified” has reference to that perfect state of righteousness which belongs not to this life.
